raw_text:
J. Van Sickle. "Quali codici d'amore nella decima egloga di
Virgilio? L'eloquio elegiaco contestualizzato nel Bueolicon liber. "
Giornate filologiche "F. Della Corte: III (Genova 2003) 31-62.
Macdonald studies the two songs in Eclogue 8 and detects a
concentric arrangement of allusions to Theocritus' Idylls. Vergil
uses the structure of the spell from Idyll 2 in both songs of Eclogue
8, but the second follows Simaetha's spell more closely. The
contest songs create tension between internal and external
imitation.
Geymonat studies the vocatives at the start and finish of the
Eclogue-Book. Shepherd and goats provide two extremes, both
associated closely with the poet, and accenting the performance.
Kraggerud accepts Heyne' s (te) in Eclogue 10.44 as inescapable
in the context of Gallus' outburst: nunc insanus amor dum te martis
in armis.

raw_text:
J. Whitehorne, "Reconciling East and West
AUMLA 103 (2005) 1-14.
Edgeworth/Stem explores Vergilian silence on points of
interpretive value for the reader: Danaids in Tartarus? Return of
Turnus' corpse? The silence reinforces the ambivalent ending of
the epic. Danaids on the balteus of Pallas compound pietas and
nefas. The counterweight of Aeneas' moral failure in killing Turnus
lies in the relief that the killing secures Rome's future. "This
revelation grinds the heart in sorrow and yet gives hope in
darkness" (166)
Konstan rejects the assumption that dementia had negative
connotations during the 1st century BC and later. The traditional
meaning is incorporated into the clupeus virtutis presented to
Augustus. Lobe offers a perceptive study of the meaning and
function of body-language around Roman epic. Alden Smith
examines the implications of the Augustan transition from rhetoric
to images to extol imperial power. Vergil is part of the
contemporary process giving primacy to imagery over speech in the
sensational epic adventures of Aeneas. Close reading of crucial
episodes reveal that Aeneas repeatedly acts on what he see rather
than what he hears. Suerbaum searches for bucolic elements in the
Aeneid. Bucolic motifs far outweigh the factor of language.
Shepherds appear not as singers but as fighters (Book 7); the
wonderland of Arcadia and Book 8 is a close reflection. The major
link is the subjective voice, the voice of compassion.

The silence reinforces the ambivalent ending of the epic. Danaids on the balteus of Pallas compound pietas and nefas. Horsfall provides a detailed commentary on Aeneid 3, called "The
Dullest Book" in earlier days. The pages shimmer with fresh
insights and a myriad of details. Students and scholars will
welcome this first detailed commentary, with text, translation, and
three indices. Dehon detects winter as the season for the African
episode. Astronomy, meteorology, and chronology allude to a
wintry atmosphere which prefigures the rupture and the lamentable
ending. Monitto favors coniugium.

raw_text:
Leah Kronenberg. "Mezentius the Epicurean." TAPA 135 (2005)
403-31.
Kronenberg argues that Mezentius, surpassing Dido, evokes
Epicurean philosophy in a manner that allows for an Epicurean
reading of the poem's action. The Epicurean element in Mezentius
helps to explain his surprising transformation from a symbol of
impietas to pietas in Books 7-10, "as well as the reverse
transformation of pius Aeneas into an impious Giant figure" (403).
"Aeneas assumes the role of impious monster just as Mezentius is
transformed into a pious philosopher." (425).
Kronenberg offers a disturbing essay. However, Vergil's characters
so often intertwine philosophic allusions with other sorts of literary
models and codes that Epicurean readings of Dido and Mezentius
cannot be proved "comprehensively wrong."

Bernays is concerned with arithmetic exactitude in poetry and
argues that Vergil followed the realm of absolute figures and
divisible ratios in the Bucolics and the Aeneid (19-25); however,
structural aspects require some drastic measures with the texts of
Eclogues 2, 8, 9 and 10. McGill probes Epigrammata Bobiensia
46, 47 and 64, Latin versions of Greek epigrams on teachers. He
argues for equivalent uses of the Iliad and Aeneid as central school
texts in Greek and Latin grammatical schools.

Gene C. Tsao. Aeneas from China . Bloomington, Indiana, 2005.
McDonald's collection of pastoral verses includes "Eclogue," an
imitation of Vergil in the most emotionally engaged of the
neoclassical pieces. Two voices debate the contemporary "troubles"
as they traverse the Castlereagh Hills above Belfast; one is about to
leave the city again. Tsao's novel is a modern version of the
Aeneas and Dido tragic romance, beginning in 1959 in the Soviet
Union and continuing into Communist China. Vasily (Zhao
Huaxing, Aeneas) has been forced to leave his beloved Sophia in
Russia where he cannot return nor can he convey her to China as
the rift between the nations broadens. Vasily senses a parallel
between Aeneas' determination to fulfill his destiny and his own
